data.title: Markets Weekly Outlook: Unclear BLS Post-Shutdown Schedule
data.description:
By Elior Manier

Week in review: Markets remain very anxious

This week finally saw the US government reopen after the longest ever government shutdown, which lasted 43 days. News of the deal initially brought a much-needed rally in stocks on Monday, but throughout the week, markets have been plagued by sudden selloffs without much explanation.

Note: The Bureau of Labor Statistics will publish the September NFP (normally published in early October) on Thursday, November 20.

As explained in our last weekly outlook, participants remain anxious on themes of high valuations, particularly as key participants like Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang issued warnings on US policies and regulations that he believes will restrain progress in AI. Huang stated that China is only "nanoseconds behind America in AI".

Many doubts remain after some warnings from private data releases, like the new weekly ADP series, which indicated an average drop of 11K jobs in the private sector in the past four weeks. This has fueled even more anxiety about how the 1.5-month "data dark age" will ultimately look.

Adding to the confusion, White House Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt announced that due to the lack of collection during the shutdown, the October jobs (NFP) and inflation (CPI) data may never be released. Leavitt claimed the data was "permanently impaired," leaving policymakers "flying blind at a critical period".

There has been a decent move higher in equities to finish the week after an even more scary open, but participants are all looking at each other to see who moves first.

Expect to receive more news from the BLS as they resume their operations again. From what it seems, they will be prioritizing November releases.

However, the September NFP is expected to be released quickly, as early as next week, as its data was collected prior to the shutdown taking effect.

Weekly Performance across Asset Classes

Cryptocurrencies see the most outflows once again in the ongoing risk-deleveraging happening throughout markets. For the rest, despite enormous volatility, most assets have mean-reverted throughout the week, leading to low weekly changes.

But for those who have been actively trading and/or watching the action unfold, everybody can confirm that the week has been far from calm.

The Week Ahead: US September NFP and many Inflation releases

Asia Pacific Markets: A focus on Japan and New Zealand combined with PBoC Rate Decision

The upcoming week for APAC markets is dominated by high-impact inflation updates, now focusing mostly on Japan and New Zealand data. AUD traders will still have to log in.

Monday starts with the release of the RBA Meeting Minutes, offering a detailed look at the Board's recent interest rate discussions and after another week of positive economic surprise (large beats in employment, delaying cuts further).

The primary domestic indicator will be the Wage Price Index (WPI), due on Tuesday evening (20:30 ET), which is the most critical measure of underlying domestic inflation pressure.

For those following China, the PBoC Interest Rate Decision on Wednesday evening (21:15 ET) will attract quite some attention.

While no change is expected, communication regarding growth and concerns will be closely watched by all participants; China released some pretty bad data the past week, particularly regarding international trade.

Japanese data sets the stage early and provides the week's biggest inflation event. Sunday night brings the preliminary Q3 GDP figures to check the economy's pulse.

However, the key macro event for JPY traders is Thursday evening's (19:30 ET) national CPI release, but this one is not as closely compared to the Tokyo CPI (releasing next week).

NZD traders will also be quite busy, with the New Zealand Producer Price Index (Q3) releasing Tuesday evening, and followed by their trade balance data on Thursday.

US, Europe and UK Markets: US September NFP, Inflation in Canada, Europe, and the UK + Some PMI spice

The upcoming week for traders is highly polarized, focusing on inflation in Europe, the UK and Canada – The picture is still unclear for US data except for a November 20 Sep NFP release!

Starting Monday, CAD traders will welcome the Consumer Price Index (CPI) at 9:30 A.M. ET. Major data continues in Europe on Wednesday (6:00 A.M. ET) with the release of the Eurozone Core HICP (CPI).

The EU will also publish crucial forward-looking sentiment figures on Friday with the HCOB PMIs (4:15-5:00 A.M. ET), giving a fresh look at economic activity.

As for the US, the week is a waiting game of surprises, particularly with the BLS uncertainty. Traders will look at the interest-rate-sensitive Housing Starts and Permits on Wednesday (8:30 A.M. ET).

The market’s real focus will be on potential past releases throughout next week, the new weekly ADP series on Tuesday, and Friday's Michigan Sentiment Survey (11:00 A.M. ET), which offers direct insight into consumer confidence and, more importantly, long-term inflation expectations.

Of course, the Swiss franc is on watch as well, with SNB Chair Thomas Jordan speaking on Friday, and any policy hint will move the safe-haven currency that has seen quite some inflows again this week.

For all market-moving economic releases and events, see the MarketPulse Economic Calendar. (High-tier data only)

Not in the picture, but keep an eye on the flurry of central bank speeches throughout the week as the final quarter rate decisions approach.

Safe Trades and enjoy your weekend!

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: PDO: 2 Things I Learned From Junk Bonds
data.description:
PDO CEF and junk bonds

I last covered the PIMCO Dynamic Income Opportunities Fund (PDO) on Sept 25 with an article titled "Why I Prefer PDO Over PDI". That article presented a comparative analysis against PDI and rated PDO as a hold for the following reasons:

My conclusion is that PDI's current price premium has already factored in the potential impacts of lower rates. From this point on, I see PDO better positioned to benefit from lower rates. I expect lower rates to have a more pronounced impact on PDO's net interest expenses and net investment income coverage.

Since then, there have been a few material changes that could impact the risk profile of this CEF. The rest of this article will detail my thoughts on the top 2 changes in my opinion: the changes in junk bond yield and also the updated outlook for future interest cuts. With PDO's heavy exposure to high-yield bonds, I keep seeing a neutral return/risk ratio from PDO and thus reiterate my holding rating. The key considerations underpinning my neutral view are twofold: 1) the yield from junk bonds, which PDO is heavily exposed, is not attractive either by historical standard or benchmarked against treasury rates, 2) the unattractiveness of PDO's current yield is understated due to the fund's relatively short history.

Let me start with the changes in junk bond yield. The next chart displays the yield from the Vanguard High-Yield Corporate Fund Investor Shares (VWEHX) since the 1980s. This is the junk bond fund with the longest history to my knowledge and thus helps to anchor the rest of the analysis against a broader context. To wit, the fund was incepted back in 1978, and it took 1~2 years for its yield to stabilize, as seen. The fund currently features a yield of 6.17% as seen, not only far below the historical average of 7.77% (the blue dotted line) but also near the lowest level (the dotted red line) in the past ~40 years.

Next, I will elaborate on what such a below-average yield means for the PDO CEF.

PDO CEF: basic fund information

To better prepare for following discussions, let me go over some basic facts of PDO (and also VWEHX too). PDO is managed by PIMCO (Pacific Investment Management Company) as a taxable bond CEF fund. It is one of the latest additions to PIMCO's income-oriented funds, with an inception date in 2021, as seen in the table below. The investing strategy of PDO, as quoted from its fund description below, focuses on high-yield assets (more on this in a minute).

PDO utilizes an opportunistic approach to pursue high conviction income-generating ideas across credit markets to seek current income as a primary objective and capital appreciation as a secondary objective. The fund will normally invest at least 25% of its total assets in mortgage-related assets issued by government agencies or other governmental entities or by private originators or issuers.

Given its relatively recent inception, the PDO fund has quite limited historical data to draw from, which motivated my search for a high-yield fund with a longer history to provide a broader context. This is where I found VWEHX, a high-yield fund incepted in 1978, as shown below.

PDO CEF: credit exposure in focus

VWEHX's focus on junk bonds and its long history provide a good amount of information for me to assess PDO because PDO is also heavily exposed to junk bonds. It is probably a good idea at this point to clarify what I mean by junk bonds. The definition I use in this article is any bond with a credit rating below the investment grade threshold. The threshold is BB and below by S&P terminology (or Ba and below Moody's terminology). VWEHX's largest exposure is toward bonds with a BB credit rating, representing almost ½ of its total assets (49.67% per its latest disclosure). Its second largest exposure is toward bonds with a B credit rating, representing another 33.8% of its total assets. The portfolio also allocates about 4.7% of its assets to bonds with a CCC credit rating or Lower. As such, the fund collectively allocated about 88% of its assets toward junk bonds and thus serves as a good proxy for these instruments in my view.

With this background, you can see that the PDO fund features similar asset allocation and credit exposure from the next chart below. Per the fund's exposure as of June 30, 2025, PDO's asset allocation is heavily concentrated in bonds, which make up an extraordinary 183.07% of the total assets (the percentage exceeds 100% due to the use of significant leverage).

In terms of the credit quality of its bond exposure, the portfolio is heavily tilted toward low-quality junk bonds. The credit rating for the largest exposure of its bond holdings (42.41%) is not rated. Among rated bond holdings, the majority fall into the junk grades as defined above, with B rated securities comprising 16.03% and BB rated securities at 6.43%. Investment-grade debt (AAA, AA, A, BBB) accounts for only a minor portion of its allocation, with AAA at 0.08%, AA at 2.83%, A at 0.86%, and BBB at 3.86%.

Given the above analysis, I believe PDO's current yield of ~11% - as attractive as it is on the surface - is not that attractive when contextualized by historical standards. As a reflection, the next chart tracks the dividend yield of PDO in the past 3 years, from 2023 to the present. As seen, the fund's average dividend yield over this period has been 11.57%. Its current value of 11.03% is below the historical average in the past 3 years by about 5%, signaling an unattractive yield.

But I believe the chart understates the unattractiveness of its current yield due to the relatively short historical data available. Judging by the fact that VWEHX's current yield is not only far below its long-term historical average but also near the lowest level in the past ~40 years, I believe the gap between PDO's current yield and its historical average would be wilder than the 5% shown here should the fund have a longer history.

Other risks and final thoughts

The second change that triggered this article is the changes in treasury rates. The picture for PDO (and/or junk bond) returns is equally concerning in my view when their yields are benchmarked by risk-free rates. To wit, the next chart shows the yield spread (YS) between Moody's Seasoned Baa Corporate Bond Yield and 10-Year Treasury Constant Maturity (BAA10Y) rates. This YS currently hovers around 1.75%, as seen, also among the lowest levels in the past 40 years or so, signaling unusual risk premium for high-yield assets relative to risk-free rates.

Looking ahead, the latest dot-plot announced by the recent Federal Open Market Committee ((FOMC) suggests a path for continued rate cuts in the next few years. These cuts, if/when materialize, could make the return/risk ratio for PDO more favorable in several ways. First, they could help to reduce the tension between junk bond yield and risk-free rates, as just mentioned. Second, they could also better support the valuation of the holdings in the PDO. And finally, given PDO's substantial use of leverage, they could also decrease the borrowing costs of the fund. To wit, the last chart shows PDO's latest leverage data as of this writing. As seen, PDO CEF employs an effective leverage of 34.7% currently.

To close, this PDO revisit is motivated by two changes since my last writing: the changes in junk bond rates and treasury rates. In particular, a close examination on junk bond yields offers two takeaways for me. First, junk bond yields, as lucrative as they appear on the surface, are not attractive by historical standards or when benchmarked against risk-free rates. Second, specific to PDO, the unattractiveness of its current yield relative to historical norms is understated due to its relatively short history.

data.title: Blackstone Secured Lending: Has The Era Of NAV And Dividend Growth Ended?
data.description:
Investment thesis

Although Business Development Companies (BDCs) are not performing well in 2025, prospects for a gradual recovery in the industry are emerging. Meanwhile, new risks are emerging (related to employment and inflation in the US economy), due to which expectations of the Fed's previous pace of interest rate cuts may change. That said, there's a debate in the financial community about how much of a negative or positive impact rate cuts could have on the profitability of financial companies like Blackstone Secured Lending Fund (BXSL). Their net interest income is highly sensitive to changes in interest rates, and could lead to a radical change in the financial profile of BDC's business. Due to the combination of growth catalysts and increased risks, my recommendation for BXSL shares is “Hold.” This solution implies maintaining the previous share of securities in the investment portfolio, thus refraining from additional positions.

Blackstone Secured Lending's strategy and growth drivers

Blackstone Secured Lending is a company managed by the reputable financial institution Blackstone (BX). As such, BXSL has access to important management tools, including a professional and experienced restructuring and underwriting team, and a huge customer base that allows it to conclude deals in the sectors of the economy that interest it. The firm conducts in-depth analysis of client corporate data and has cross-selling capabilities to support decision-making on portfolio development.

In spite of the current period of stagnation in the BDC sector, Blackstone Secured Lending appears to be one of the most reliable companies in terms of business development, boasting strong fundamentals and financial results, from earnings to interest rate coverage. Due to the gradual growth of net asset value (NAV), the management of BXSL manages to maintain a high level of competitiveness of the financial company, creating a certain premium in the valuation of its shares. The major share of Blackstone Secured Lending's portfolio is distributed among 301 companies. The share of each does not exceed 3% of all assets. The main industries for BXSL's work with clients are Software (20% of the total share), Health Care Providers & Services (10% of the total share), and Professional Services (10% of the total share).

Blackstone Secured Lending also has a high-quality portfolio. Approximately 97.5% of its loans are secured by collateral, and its low level of accrued interest, strong diversification, and high level of liquidity ensure its conservatism and reliability. Comparing an indicator such as collateralization of debt obligations, those figures are 76% and 59% for companies such as Blue Owl Capital Corp. (OBDC) and Ares Capital (ARCC). That's 22% and 39% less than BXSL. With a highly volatile environment, macroeconomic turbulence, and changes in the Fed's monetary policy, that's one of the key catalysts that could drive BXSL's growth. Additional income and stable NAV values ensure dividend security while mitigating the risk of financial losses.

Further catalysts for Blackstone Secured Lending's growth include its BBB- credit rating with a positive outlook from S&P and Baa2 rating from Moody's. First of all, these reflect the fact that BXSL is a financially sound company with credit obligations at a safe level (from the perspective of institutional investors). The positive outlook in S&P's rating demonstrates that the rating agency sees factors that indicate a trend toward improvement in the quality of the financial company's loan portfolio. That is why a revision of the rating to BBB is possible in the future. One more practical thing about credit ratings for BDC companies, like Blackstone Secured Lending, is that it can help them get cheaper loans. Thus, BXSL can boost its income from the spread between the cost of raising capital and the cost of lending it out.

One more important thing to think about when checking out how cool BXSL is as an investment is its dividend policy. Currently, dividends are $0.77 per share, which corresponds to a yield of 11.3%. Its net investment income allows it to cover dividends per share, with a dividend coverage ratio of 106%. The ratio increased by 6% (from 100%) compared to Q2 2025. At the same time, however, there is a negative trend in the decline of the dividend coverage ratio from 2022-2023. Regardless, however, this dividend coverage is a key factor in determining how sustainable the current dividend policy is, specifically the company's ability to pay dividends at the same level as before.

Blackstone Secured Lending Q3 2025 Earnings Report

Blackstone Secured Lending reported positive results for Q3 2025, exceeding analysts' expectations. The company's GAAP net interest income for the third quarter was $0.82, which was $0.04 higher than the consensus forecast of analysts, and total investment income was $358.56 million (+4.5% year-on-year), which was $5.14 million higher than the consensus forecast of analysts. Meanwhile, analysts are setting a more negative consensus forecast for Q4 2025, anticipating a 5.14% decline in EPS compared to the same period a year earlier.

At that, analyzing data for Q3 2025, net interest income decreased from $0.91 to $0.82 (-9.9% y/y). When analyzing the NAV per share, it should be noted that compared to the beginning of 2025, the figure decreased from $27.39 to $27.15. It coincides with a decrease in net asset growth as a result of operating activities. Thus, according to data for Q3 2025, the indicator amounted to $132.4 million, whereas a year earlier it was $151.7 million. Meanwhile, even the value for Q2 2025 was significantly higher – $155 million. More details on Blackstone Secured Lending's financial results for Q3 2025 are provided below.

Also within the framework of the conference call on the results of the third quarter reporting, the management of the company announced prospects related to maintaining expectations of high transaction activity, due to which asset turnover will increase and spreads will remain attractive compared to traditional financing products. It was noted that the dividend level will be monitored in light of the impact of interest rate cuts on the financial company's profits. Indeed, if the amount of dividend payments that are not 100% covered remains unchanged, there will be a decline in the competitiveness and financial stability of the business.

Besides, Blackstone Secured Lending's credit risk management team has reported observations that the default rate in the credit market is declining. Considering that the company's loan portfolio is protected by senior secured loans with first claim rights, cases of default pose an even smaller threat to the stability of the business. Meanwhile, separate attention is paid to monitoring the risks of individual clients. For example, the Medallia case was one of the key factors that led to a decline in net asset value per share in Q3 2025.

Blackstone Secured Lending Assessment

To evaluate the investment attractiveness of Blackstone Secured Lending, I propose first analyzing the BDC sector through the dynamics of the VanEck BDC Income ETF (BIZD), which is showing a steady downward trend. To do so, let's turn to technical analysis of the 1W time frame chart. I am interested in this approach to evaluating BXSL because analyzing the dynamics of the entire sector allows us to determine the market conditions of the industry to which Blackstone Secured Lending's business profile belongs. Additionally, analyzing BIZD confirms the steady downward trend that began in February 2025.

It was in this period that the price reached the resistance zone, which had been formed at equal high levels (EQH). Its test did not allow the price to break above, due to which the quotes headed downwards for a raid on the liquidity of the local minimum of March 2023. Consequently, a new support level formed at $13.50, and testing it in October 2025 suggests that the asset may recover to the upper boundary of the downward trend channel. However, I believe that the liquidity level of $12.93 remains relevant, the raid of which may become the target for a renewed wave of downward price dynamics.

Turning to an analysis of the comparative growth dynamics of Blackstone Secured Lending shares and its competitors in the BDC sector in 2025 (since the beginning of the year), it should be noted that BXSL is showing the worst results (a decline of 13.84%), followed by Hercules Capital (HTGC) securities with a decline of -12.02%. Main Street Capital Corporation (MAIN) shares are the only asset showing positive growth (+12.88%). This dynamic confirms the results of the BIZD ETF technical analysis that the market is in a long-term downtrend.

Risks of Blackstone Secured Lending in assessing their prospects

In assessing the risks of Blackstone Secured Lending, it's important to pay attention to how changes in the Fed's interest rate might affect it. If central bank leaders apply these decisions in the next 3-6 months, it might cause the company's dividends to go down. This would be because the dividend coverage ratio would fall below 100%. The forecast for the meeting on December 10, 2025, expects a 59.9% probability that the interest rate will be reduced by 25 basis points. Therefore, the probability of this scenario is quite high, and results may be noticeable as early as the end of Q4 2025 or Q1 2026.

As BXSL's business model is built on BDC, its main activity depends on venture lending at floating rates. Consequently, a change in the Fed's interest rate will also affect the interest rates on debt for the companies and projects being financed. The primary impact of a decrease in interest rates will be on net investment income. This is the indicator that determines the fair value of BXSL.

Basically, financing programs for clients are structured as contracts with floating interest rates tied to the SOFR indicator. A reduction in the Fed's interest rate leads to a reduction in the SOFR rate. From September 14, 2025, to September 17, 2025, the SOFR rate fell from 4.51% to 4.14% due to the Fed's decision. After that, the next decision to lower the Fed rate led to a further decrease in the SOFR rate to 3.91%. The result is that the spread may decrease, and with it, net investment income. As a consequence, the company will have to make decisions aimed at reducing dividend payments, which will affect the assessment of the investment attractiveness of BXSL shares.

As an outcome, these factors negatively impact analysts' forecast revisions. Over the last three months, there were 3 EPS revisions, all of which were negative. Consequently, annual EPS is expected to be $2.96 in 2026 and $2.71 in 2027. The annualized EPS decline will be -7.89% and -8.3%. Meanwhile, the consensus forecast for 2026 was formed by the estimates of 11 analysts, which can't be called insufficient attention to BXSL securities. This tendency is negative and reflects the fact that analysts do not predict growth in the company's financial results for the next two years. The main reasons for this revision are expectations of a reduction in the Fed's interest rate.

Conclusion

In summary, the results of the fundamental analysis of Blackstone Secured Lending allow us to assess the average investment attractiveness of this asset. On one side, BXSL has competitive advantages that show how financially solid and stable the company's asset portfolio is. On the other hand, there is a high valuation of financial results, net investment income, as well as net asset value per share. On the other hand, Blackstone Secured Lending may face a number of problems caused by external factors by early 2026, which will lower spreads and reduce net investment income. As an outcome, potentially, the decision to reduce dividend payments in the next quarterly reports may be considered, causing investors' assessment to deteriorate. With additional revisions to analysts' forecasts lowering stock valuations for 2026-2027, the probability of significant growth in BXSL's market capitalization is declining.

data.title: October Market Recap: When A Late-Cycle Economy Meets An Early-Cycle Tech Boom
data.description:
A late-cycle economy is colliding with an early-cycle tech boom. Debt is piling up just as disruption accelerates, and the U.S. is funding a historic wave of AI, automation, and energy investment with record leverage.

Debt Meets Disruption

Congratulations to Zohran Mamdani, New York City’s new mayor — bringing socialism to the capital of capitalism.

It’s the perfect symbol of a world straining under inflation, inequality, and automation. Populism was inevitable. Now we’ll see how far it goes.

We’re in a strange place — a late-cycle economy colliding with an early-cycle innovation boom. Debt is piling up just as disruption accelerates. The U.S. is simultaneously at the end of a credit cycle and the beginning of a historic investment cycle in AI, automation, and energy. Debt meets disruption — and that collision will define the next decade.

The Three Phases of AI

We see AI unfolding in three clear phases:

  1. Builders – the companies creating and powering the technology.
  2. Adopters – the companies using it to drive efficiency and cut costs.
  3. Automators – the convergence of AI and robotics that will redefine labor and productivity.

Most of the world is still in phase 1. A few leaders are moving into phase 2. And the first signs of phase 3 are beginning to appear.

Phase 1 - Builders Still Building

The builders are proving both the promise and the price of AI — and investors are starting to question what the bill looks like.

  • Microsoft: $77 billion in revenue and $30 billion in profit, but investors focused on rising AI-infrastructure costs and cautious guidance.
  • Meta: 26% revenue growth, but the stock fell after management warned that AI spending will remain elevated — a reminder that building the future isn’t cheap.

Both stocks declined after reporting. The "price" of AI isn’t just the chips and servers — it’s the strain on cash flow, the pressure on margins, and the patience it demands from investors.

This is what Phase 1 looks like in real time: the world’s largest technology firms pouring capital into compute, cloud, and data infrastructure while markets demand to know when it pays off.

The story is shifting from growth at any cost to growth with accountability. The “build” phase is still running, but markets are starting to ask the right question: when does all this spending translate into profits?

MSFT and META Dip: AI Spending Strains Balance Sheets

Phase 2 - Adopters Reshape Work

While investors questioned the Builders’ soaring budgets, the Adopters are showing how AI can improve efficiency — and reshape work in the process.

Amazon, the second-largest U.S. employer, offered one of the clearest examples in October. Its shares rose about 11% after earnings as revenue climbed roughly 13% year-over-year to $180 billion and profit surged nearly 40%. Growth in AWS — up ~20% to $33 billion — reassured investors that Amazon is turning AI investments into results.

Amazon Rises: AI-Adoption Efficiencies Enhances Outlook

Earlier in the year, CEO Andy Jassy had noted that AI would allow Amazon to operate more efficiently and reduce the need for some corporate roles. That message has since become visible in the data: the company has announced about 14k corporate job cuts, with plans to eliminate as many as 30k positions. Its own automation and machine-learning tools are increasingly handling tasks once done by analysts, managers, and recruiters. Investors rewarded evidence that adoption is improving productivity and lowering costs, not just driving spending.

Accenture followed a similar path. It’s a business built to cut costs and modernize — exactly what AI does. The irony is rich: the company that spent decades automating people out of jobs is now being automated itself. Earlier this year, it announced 11,000 layoffs as part of a pivot toward AI, but the threat runs deeper. Generative AI can now do much of what Accenture sells — writing code, analyzing data, and redesigning workflows — potentially cutting the firm out entirely. The stock is down nearly 30% this year, as the market starts to price in that risk.

The data echo the headlines. A recent Economist analysis, based on a study of 300,000 companies, found that firms integrating AI into daily operations are already reducing junior-level roles 7.7% faster than those that haven’t, while senior positions remain stable. It’s a subtle but telling signal: AI isn’t replacing managers yet — it’s replacing the people who once worked for them. This is a peek behind the curtain of what’s coming — the early outlines of a labor market being quietly rewritten. Phase 2 isn’t a corporate trend; it’s the new operating model.

AI Has Heightened Impact for Junior Employees

Phase 3 - Automation Takes the Floor

Some companies have entered phase 3, but what’s experimental today will soon be standard. Inside Amazon’s warehouses, Blue Jay — a new AI-powered robotic arm — now picks, sorts, and consolidates roughly 75% of items, guided by Project Eluna. This AI system manages workflow and predicts bottlenecks. For now, this is early-stage automation at scale — limited to logistics — but it’s a preview of what’s next. When the second-largest employer in America builds machines that can replace both managers and movers, it’s more than efficiency — it’s evolution. Amazon is in the early innings of phase 3, but its scale ensures the ripple effects won’t stay contained.

AI will bring extraordinary productivity gains — but also widespread job displacement. This will be a structural shift, not a temporary shock. The U.S. will eventually be forced to support displaced workers through larger social spending. Add that to the tab.

Debt, Liquidity, and the Opt-Out

While technology races ahead, the financial system keeps falling behind. The U.S. is choking on debt and entering a massive capital-expenditure cycle to win at all costs — in technology, efficiency, and energy independence. Fiscal restraint doesn’t fit. Gold and Bitcoin have both corrected sharply after extraordinary runs. Bitcoin, which topped $125,000 earlier this year, recently fell back below $100,000. Gold hit $4,356 before slipping under $4,000. These are meaningful moves, but they’re not signs of weakness — they’re the natural volatility of assets built on conviction rather than cash flow.

We said last month that gold’s bull market would come with higher volatility — and it has. The long-term setup hasn’t changed: the world is spending to fund progress, not to pay down debt. In that environment, scarce assets remain the opt-outs — stores of value that can’t be printed to fund excess.

And here’s our view: in six months, we believe you’ll wish you bought these assets at today’s prices. We also believe they could go lower first. The only thing we know for sure is that trying to pick the bottom is a loser’s game. Don’t be cute. Nibble at it. Volatility is your friend.

Late-Cycle Credit Risk

Jamie Dimon recently likened the emergence of bad loans to spotting cockroaches — if you see one, there are more. He’s right, but this isn’t about a few bad borrowers. It’s about being late cycle, when the economy still looks strong, markets are confident, and the ugly side of excess starts to show.

The worst loans are made at the best of times. Private credit has exploded to more than $1.6 trillion, fueled by investors chasing yield and convinced that risk has been engineered out of the system. Regional banks, meanwhile, are being squeezed by higher funding costs and a wall of commercial real estate refinancing that doesn’t work at current rates. The market is catching on. The S&P® Regional Banks Select Industry Index (SPSIRBKT) and the MarketVector Alternative Asset Managers Index (MVAALTTR) have both materially underperformed the broader market since September, when these risks began to surface. Together, they capture two ends of the same problem — the lenders that fund private credit and the funds that rely on that funding.

This isn’t 2008 — bank capital is stronger — but the pattern is familiar. Risk migrates, leverage builds, and when the music stops, losses move from private to public balance sheets. Credit stress becomes fiscal stress.

Lenders Lag the Broad Market

The GENIUS Act and Digital Demand

Amid all of this, new forces are shaping liquidity in unexpected ways.

The GENIUS Act is quietly creating structural buyers of dollars and Treasuries by requiring stablecoins to hold reserves in short-term U.S. debt.

That policy change is already visible in the data. The market capitalizations of USDT and USDC have surged since the election, accelerating as regulatory clarity improved. More stablecoin use means more Treasury demand — digital dollars backed by real T-bills.

It’s ironic that digital assets were once viewed as a risk to the dollar. Now stablecoins are extending the reach of the dollar in a world seemingly desperate to de-dollarize. In that regard, the GENIUS Act really was genius.

Stablecoins Accelerate Demand for USD

Macro themes we’re watching:

Debt

AI’s Three Phases

De-Dollarization
Fiscal excess is fueling innovation and instability. Builders spend, Adopters save, Automators replace. Stores of value to hedge against deficits, debt, and geopolitics.

Today’s predominant macro forces are driving the key themes and exposures in VanEck’s models, including the core allocation of the VanEck Wealth Builder Plus Portfolios. The allocations below are representative of the Moderate Portfolio.

Important Disclosures

data.title: Treasury Yields Snapshot: November 14, 2025
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The yield on the 10-year note finished November 14, 2025, at 4.14%. Meanwhile, the 2-year note ended at 3.62%, and the 30-year note ended at 4.74%.

The chart below overlays the daily performance of several Treasury bonds, starting from the pre-recession equity market peaks, along with the Federal Funds Rate (FFR) since 2007.

This next table shows the highs and lows of yields and the Federal Funds Rate since 2007.

A Long-Term Look at the 10-Year Treasury Yield

Here is a long-term view of the 10-year yield starting in 1965, well before the 1973 oil embargo that triggered the era of 'stagflation' (economic stagnation coupled with inflation)

Inverted Yield Curve

An inverted yield curve is when longer-term Treasury yields are lower than their shorter-term counterparts. The next chart displays the latest 10-2 spread. Typically, the spread turns negative for a period before rising again prior to recessions, as illustrated in the four recessions shown on this chart. For this reason, the 10-2 spread is widely considered a reliable leading indicator for recessions. The lead time between a negative spread and the onset of a recession varies, with recessions beginning anywhere from 18 to 92 weeks after the spread goes negative.

One false positive is seen in 1998, where the spread briefly went negative without leading to a recession. In the case of the 2009 recession, the spread went negative multiple times before rising again. Most recently, the spread was continuously negative from July 5, 2022, to August 26, 2024. The last time the spread was negative was on September 5, 2024.

If we consider the first negative spread date as the starting point, the average lead time to a recession is 48 weeks, or about eleven months. If we instead use the last positive spread date before a recession, the average lead time is 18.5 weeks, or about 4.25 months.

For another perspective on the yield curve, the 10-3mo spread below uses an even shorter-term maturity. The lead time to recessions based on this spread (after it turns negative) ranges from 34 to 69 weeks. Like the 10-2 spread, we see the same false positive in 1998, as well as multiple instances of the spread turning negative before rising again ahead of the 2009 recession. Recently, the spread was negative from October 25, 2022, to December 12, 2024. Since February 26, the spread has swung back and forth from positive to negative territory.

If we consider the first negative spread date as the starting point, the average lead time to a recession is 48 weeks, or about eleven months. If we instead use the last positive spread date after the spread had been negative, the average lead time is 13 weeks, or about three months.

The 30-Year Fixed Rate Mortgage

The Federal Funds Rate influences the cost of borrowing for banks. When the Fed increases this rate, banks often raise their lending rates, which can impact mortgage rates, among other things. Therefore, a rising FFR generally leads to higher mortgage rates, and vice versa. However, this was not the case recently when the Fed began their rate-cutting cycle in September and mortgage rates moved in the opposite direction. With that said, mortgage rates have recently been on the decline while the Fed has held rates steady. The latest Freddie Mac Weekly Primary Mortgage Market Survey put the 30-year fixed rate at 6.24%, one of its lowest levels in over a year.

Now let's see the 10-year against the S&P 500 with some notes on Federal Reserve intervention. Fed policy has been a major influence on market behavior.

For a long-term view of weekly Treasury yields, also focusing on the 10-year, see our latest Treasury Yields in Perspective update.

ETFs associated with Treasuries include: Vanguard 0-3 Month Treasury Bill ETF (VBIL), Vanguard Intermediate-Term Treasury ETF (VGIT), and Vanguard Long-Term Treasury ETF (VGLT).
